Reorganize Information Hierarchy: To improve the information hierarchy and provide a clear path from overview to detail, make the map pins interactive. Currently, the pins on the map are static, preventing users from learning more about each location. When a user taps on any of the red map pins, a bottom sheet component should slide up from the bottom of the screen, displaying the name and address of the selected distributor location.

To offer users a more structured way to browse information, introduce an alternative view to the map. The current map-only presentation makes it difficult to scan all distributors quickly. Add a segmented control with "Map" and "List" options within the red header bar, to the right of the "Distributors" title. Tapping "List" should replace the map with a vertically scrollable list of all distributors, with each entry showing the distributor's name and city.

To better organize the visual information on the map and reduce clutter, group dense collections of pins. The current view shows all pins individually, causing them to overlap and become unselectable in areas with many distributors. Implement pin clustering, so that when the map is zoomed out, multiple nearby pins are consolidated into a single, circular marker displaying the number of distributors it represents. Tapping a cluster marker should zoom the map in to reveal the individual pins.
Refine Layout & Spacing: The elements within the red header bar are not vertically centered, creating a slight visual imbalance. To improve the header's alignment and polish, vertically center the hamburger menu icon in the top-left, and the logo and "Distributors" text in the top-center, within the bounds of the red header.

The white "my location" button in the top-right corner is positioned too close to the header and the right edge of the screen, making the layout feel cramped. Increase the margin between this button and the red header bar above it, and also increase the margin between the button and the right edge of the screen, so that the top and right margins are equal.

The hamburger menu icon in the top-left of the red header bar is positioned too close to the edge of the screen, which can make it difficult to tap accurately. To create better visual balance and improve usability, add horizontal padding to the left of this icon, moving it slightly to the right so it is not flush with the screen edge.
Clarify Function & State: To clarify which distributor a user has selected, the map pins need a distinct active state. Currently, all pins are visually identical, so when one is tapped, there is no feedback to indicate it is the focus, creating ambiguity for the user. When a user taps one of the red, teardrop-shaped map pins, it should change color to blue and increase in size by 25% to signify it is selected, while any previously selected pin reverts to its original state.

The "center on my location" button's state is currently unclear, as it looks the same whether the map is centered on the user or not. To provide clear feedback on the map's state, the button's appearance should reflect its function's status. Change the white square button with a target icon in the top-right corner so that when the map view is centered on the user's location, the target icon becomes filled with the same red color as the header bar.

In areas with a high density of locations, the overlapping map pins make it impossible to see how many distributors are present or to select a specific one. This obscures information and makes interaction difficult. Implement clustering for the red map pins: when multiple pins are too close to be displayed individually, group them into a single circular marker that displays the number of pins it represents, and which un-clusters into individual pins upon being tapped or zoomed in on.
Add or Surface Functionality: To allow users to find distributors in a specific area without having to manually pan and zoom, add a search function. In the red header bar, place a white magnifying glass icon to the right of the "Distributors" title. Tapping this icon should transform the header, replacing the title and icons with a text input field for searching locations.

To help users quickly scan and identify the distributors visible on the map, surface a summarized list view. Add a partially collapsed bottom sheet that displays the title "Nearby Distributors" and a draggable handle. When the user drags this sheet upwards, it should expand to reveal a scrollable list of all the distributors currently visible in the map viewport, with each list item showing the distributor's name and address.

To allow users to refine the map results based on specific criteria, introduce a filtering capability. Add a new "Filter" icon button (using a funnel icon) in the top red app bar, to the right of the "Distributors" title. Tapping this icon should open a modal or a right-hand drawer that contains a list of filter options, such as "Open now" or by specific product types, each with a checkbox.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: The header currently contains both a logo and a text title, which is redundant information taking up valuable space. To simplify the header and reduce this clutter, remove the white, diamond-shaped logo located in the red header bar between the menu icon and the "Distributors" title. After removing the logo, horizontally center the "Distributors" title within the header bar.

On the map, several location pins are clustered so tightly that they overlap, making it difficult to distinguish or select individual distributors. To reduce this clutter, implement pin clustering for the red map markers. When multiple pins are in close proximity at the current zoom level, they should be grouped into a single circular marker that displays the number of pins it represents. Tapping on a cluster should zoom the map to a level where the individual pins within that cluster are displayed separately.

The default map style includes numerous labels for cities, parks, and roads, which creates visual noise that competes for attention with the distributor location pins. To simplify the view and help users focus, apply a minimalist custom map style. This new style should use a more muted color palette and reduce the visibility or remove non-essential map labels, such as parks and minor roads, to ensure the red distributor pins are the most prominent elements on the screen.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more cohesive visual language, the floating action button for location should align with the app's primary color scheme instead of using a generic default style. The current location button is the white square with a gray crosshair icon in the top-right corner of the map. Change this button to be circular, with a solid red background matching the header bar and a white crosshair icon inside.

The generic red map pins do not reinforce the app's brand identity, representing a missed opportunity for consistent visual branding throughout the user interface. The components to change are the seven red, teardrop-shaped map pins with white circles in the center, which are scattered across the map. Replace these generic pins with a custom icon that uses the app's diamond-shaped logo from the header as its primary graphic.

The solid fill style of the hamburger menu icon is inconsistent with the outlined style of the adjacent app logo, creating a visual disconnect between the two primary header elements. The component to be revised is the three-bar hamburger menu icon in the top-left corner of the header. Change the icon's style from solid white bars to outlined bars, using a white stroke of the same weight as the outline of the diamond-shaped logo to its right.
Improve Readability & Accessibility: To improve accessibility, the contrast between the white text and the red background in the top navigation bar needs to be increased. The red header bar at the top of the screen, which contains the menu icon, logo, and the "Distributors" title, currently fails to meet WCAG AA contrast standards. Update the red background color to a darker shade, such as #D50000, to ensure the contrast ratio with the white text and icons is at least 4.5:1.

The icon inside the "my location" button is difficult to see due to low contrast with its background, hindering its recognizability. The light grey target icon within the square white floating button in the top-right corner of the map should be made more prominent. Change the color of the target icon from light grey to a much darker grey, like #5F6368, to meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio for graphical elements.

The white text on the red highway route markers on the map is not sufficiently legible, making it hard for users to identify major roads. The red rectangular shields for highways like "A4", "A26", and "A6" have poor text contrast. Apply a custom map style to change the background color of these highway shields to a darker red, ensuring the white text is clearly readable and meets accessibility contrast guidelines.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: The prominent "DIESEL #2" price in the top card is redundant, as it is repeated in the "Fuel" section below, creating unnecessary clutter in the primary information block. To improve the information hierarchy, remove the large price text "4.68⁹" and its "DIESEL #2" label from the top-right corner of the main store details card. This change consolidates all fuel prices within the dedicated "Fuel" section, making it the single source of truth and increasing the scannability of the store's address.

The current layout mixes static store information (amenities like "Arby's") with interactive user controls ("Call," "Directions"), disrupting the informational hierarchy. To create a clearer distinction between information and actions, reorder the elements within the top card. Move the row of four circular action buttons ("Call," "Directions," "Info," "Share") to be positioned directly above the row containing the "Arby's" and "Open 24/7" amenities.

For a driver, fuel pricing is often the most important information after the location itself, but the detailed price list is currently positioned in a secondary card below general amenities. To better align the layout with user priorities, reorganize the main content cards. Move the entire "Fuel" card (starting with the "Fuel" header) to be directly below the store name and address block, and position the card containing the amenities ("Arby's," "Open 24/7") and action buttons ("Call," "Directions," etc.) below the "Fuel" card.
Refine Layout & Spacing: To improve scannability and visual hierarchy in the fuel price list, the price for multi-line items should align with the primary label. For the "DIESEL #2" row in the "Fuel" card, which currently has the price "4.68⁹" vertically centered against both lines of text on the left, adjust the vertical alignment. The price "4.68⁹" should be vertically centered with only the "DIESEL #2" text, ignoring the "B20, ULSD" sub-label for alignment.

The row of four action icons feels vertically cramped, reducing its prominence and making the layout feel dense. To create better separation and breathing room, increase the vertical spacing around this row. For the container holding the "Call," "Directions," "Info," and "Share" icon buttons, increase the top and bottom padding by 8dp.

The primary information block at the top of the screen has a slightly unbalanced feel due to the centered vertical alignment between the two-line address on the left and the large price on the right. To create a more stable and organized structure, adjust the alignment within this block. Vertically align the address block ("I-5, Exit 434/Sperry Ave" and "Patterson, CA") and the price block ("4.68⁹" and "DIESEL #2") to the top of their shared container, so their top edges are flush.
Clarify Function & State: To reduce ambiguity, the function of the prominent price in the header should be explicitly linked to the detailed fuel prices below. Currently, the text "DIESEL #2" appears under the price "4.68⁹" in the main store details card. Change this label to "Commercial Diesel #2" to clarify that this is the price for the fuel type selected in the "COMMERCIAL" tab of the Fuel section.

The interactive state of the "COMMERCIAL," "CASUAL," and "PROPANE" fuel type selectors is not as clear as it could be, as the unselected tabs appear as plain text. To clarify that these are tappable controls, the text labels for the unselected tabs ("CASUAL" and "PROPANE") in the "Fuel" section should have their opacity increased from its current muted state to 100%, making them visually consistent with other interactive text elements.

The function of the address text is unclear, as it doesn't appear to be interactive. To clarify that this element can be tapped to open a map, the address text "I-5, Exit 434/Sperry Ave" in the main details card should be changed to the app's primary accent color (the same yellow used for the icons below) to indicate it is a tappable link.
Add or Surface Functionality: To help users easily plan their trips by seeing all available services, a dedicated entry point for amenities should be added. The current UI only shows two amenities ("Arby's" and "Open 24/7"). Add a new tappable row directly below the "Open 24/7" entry, containing a list-style icon and the text "View All Amenities," which will navigate the user to a detailed amenities page.

Users may need to use the store's address in other applications, but there is currently no simple way to copy it. To add this functionality, place a small "copy" icon to the right of the address block (containing "I-5, Exit 434/Sperry Ave" and "Patterson, CA"). When a user taps this icon, the full address should be copied to their clipboard, and a toast notification reading "Address copied" should briefly appear at the bottom of the screen.

The fuel specifications listed, such as "B20, ULSD," may be unclear to some users, potentially causing confusion when selecting fuel. To surface this information, add a small, circular information ("i") icon to the right of the "B20, ULSD" text under the "DIESEL #2" heading. Tapping this icon should display a modal or bottom sheet overlay that provides a clear definition for each acronym.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: The price of Diesel #2 is displayed redundantly in both the top store information card and the "Fuel" section below, adding unnecessary clutter. To simplify the interface, remove the large white price text "4.68⁹" and its corresponding gray "DIESEL #2" sub-text from the top-right corner of the main store details card. The distance information ("83.3 mi") and its location pin icon should remain in that top-right area.

The purpose of the fuel pricing card is made self-evident by its content, including the fuel type tabs and price list, making the "Fuel" heading redundant. To reduce visual clutter, remove the entire header row within the "Fuel" card that contains the fuel pump icon and the white text label "Fuel". The tabs for "COMMERCIAL", "CASUAL", and "PROPANE" should become the new top of this card.

The row of four action icons creates a visually dense block of controls, and not all actions are of equal importance to the user. To simplify this section and prioritize primary actions, consolidate the "Info" and "Share" icon buttons, which are located in the middle of the screen. Replace these two buttons with a single, identically styled circular button that uses a standard "more" icon (three vertical dots), which will reveal the "Info" and "Share" options when tapped.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more cohesive visual language for icons, the styling of the amenity icons should be updated to match the action icons. The shopping cart icon next to "Arby's" and the clock icon next to "Open 24/7" currently use a light gray outline style. Change these icons to a solid fill using the same yellow as the "Call" and "Directions" icons below them.

The typographic hierarchy for secondary store details is currently inconsistent. The street address ("I-5, Exit 434/Sperry Ave") located under the store name is bold, while other details like the city, restaurant, and hours are regular weight. Change the font weight of the "I-5, Exit 434/Sperry Ave" text from bold to regular to visually align it with the other secondary information lines.

The text label for "DIESEL #2" is presented with two different font weights, creating a stylistic inconsistency. The "DIESEL #2" label in the top-right of the main card, underneath the large price, is currently regular weight. Update this text to be bold, matching the font weight of the "DIESEL #2" heading within the "Fuel" section below.
Improve Readability & Accessibility: To improve legibility for users with low vision, increase the contrast of the secondary text elements. The light gray text used for the location "Patterson, CA", the amenity "Arby's", the hours "Open 24/7", and the fuel detail "B20, ULSD" has insufficient contrast against the dark background. Change the color of these text elements to a lighter gray, ensuring a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 against the background.

To enhance price readability, especially for users with visual impairments, adjust the styling of the fractional cent digit. The superscript "9" in the prices "4.68⁹" and "3.98⁹" is too small and thin to be easily read. Increase the font weight of the superscript digit to medium or semi-bold and increase its font size by 2pt to make it more prominent and legible.

For better accessibility and to ensure all options are clearly visible, increase the contrast of the inactive tab labels in the "Fuel" section. The "CASUAL" and "PROPANE" text labels are a muted gray with low contrast, making them difficult to read. Update the text color for these inactive tabs to a lighter gray (e.g., #BDBDBD) to achieve a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 with the dark background.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: To create a stronger connection between the course title and its content, the secondary metadata should be repositioned. Move the section containing the "Teacher: Sebastian Orth" line and the "Category: Meditation & Mindfulness" line (including its thumbnail image) from its current location below the main title to be directly underneath the "Description" and "Curriculum" tab bar. This change will place the interactive tabs immediately after the main title, clarifying the page's primary information hierarchy and content structure.

The current layout splits primary and secondary actions across two header bars, which confuses the information hierarchy. To prioritize core navigation, consolidate the two headers by removing the yellow-gold bar containing the Facebook and Instagram icons. Relocate the shopping cart, user profile, and hamburger menu icons from the white header bar into the top-right corner of the page, maintaining the back arrow's position in the top-left.

The "Teacher" and "Category" information is currently presented as two separate vertical blocks, fragmenting the course's metadata. To create a more scannable and cohesive information unit, reorganize these elements into a single horizontal line. Place the text "Teacher: Sebastian Orth" and "Category: Meditation & Mindfulness" side-by-side below the main title "Guided Meditation: Abundance", separated by a middle dot (•), and remove the small thumbnail image that is currently next to the category.
Refine Layout & Spacing: The current mix of centered alignment for the teacher's name and left alignment for the category information creates a visually disjointed and unbalanced layout. Left-align the "Teacher" label and "Sebastian Orth" name, which are currently centered beneath the main page title, so their left edge aligns with the left edge of the category thumbnail image below them.

The three icons in the header are tightly spaced, which can make them difficult to tap accurately on mobile devices and gives the section a cramped feel. In the header bar, increase the horizontal spacing between the shopping cart icon, the user profile icon, and the hamburger menu icon by 8px to create more visual separation and improve their touch targets.

The vertical spacing between the main title, the teacher's name, and the category information is excessive, making these related pieces of information feel disconnected from each other. Reduce the top margin of the "Teacher: Sebastian Orth" text block by 50%, and similarly, reduce the top margin of the "Category: Meditation & Mindfulness" block by 50% to create a more cohesive information hierarchy.
Clarify Function & State: To clarify its interactive state, the inactive "Curriculum" tab's text color should be changed. The current light grey text on a light grey background has low contrast, making it hard to read and potentially appear disabled. Modify the "Curriculum" tab button, located to the right of the selected "Description" tab, by changing its text color from the current light grey to a darker grey, such as #555555, to ensure it is legible and clearly identifiable as a selectable option.

To clarify that the teacher's name is an interactive link, its styling needs to be updated. Currently, the text "Sebastian Orth" under the "Teacher" label appears as static, bold text, giving no indication that it is tappable. Change the "Sebastian Orth" text to use the app's standard link styling by making it the same purple color as the back arrow in the header and adding an underline, clearly communicating its function as a link to the teacher's profile.

To clarify the function of the social media icons, they need to be visually distinguished as interactive elements. The Facebook and Instagram icons in the top gold bar currently lack affordance and could be mistaken for static branding. To indicate they are tappable links, enclose each icon within a 32x32 pixel circular button with a transparent background and a subtle, 1px light grey border (#DDDDDD).
Add or Surface Functionality: To allow users to save a course for later without adding it to their cart, add a "favorite" button. In the main content area, place an outlined heart icon to the right of the main title, "Guided Meditation: Abundance". Tapping this icon should toggle its state to a solid-filled heart and add the course to a user's "Favorites" list.

The duration of the meditation is a critical piece of information that is currently missing, preventing users from quickly assessing if the course fits their needs. To surface this, add a new "Duration" information line in the section below the main title. This new line should appear below "Category: Meditation & Mindfulness" and be styled identically, displaying text such as "Duration: 20 minutes".

To help users make a more informed purchase decision, add a "preview" function. In the space between the "Category" information and the "Description/Curriculum" tabs, insert a new, centered button labeled "Play Preview". This button should be styled as a secondary action (e.g., white background, thin gray border, and gray text) and should play a short audio sample of the meditation when tapped.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: To reduce header clutter and focus the user on the page content, the topmost social media bar should be removed. This bar is a distracting global element on a page that requires user concentration. Remove the entire yellow header bar containing the Facebook and Instagram icons, making the navigation bar with the cart icon the new top-most element on the screen.

The labels "Teacher" and "Category" are redundant as the information presented is self-evident from context, adding unnecessary text that increases cognitive load. Identify the gray text label "Teacher" located above the name "Sebastian Orth" and the gray text label "Category" located to the left of the "Meditation & Mindfulness" text. Remove both of these labels, leaving only the teacher's name, the thumbnail, and the category name.

The presence of three separate icons in the main navigation bar creates unnecessary complexity. To simplify this and reduce visual noise, the shopping cart and user profile icons should be consolidated. Remove the shopping cart icon and the user profile icon from the main navigation bar. Their corresponding functions should be added as new line items within the drawer that opens upon tapping the hamburger menu icon.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more cohesive color palette for interactive elements, update the color of the back arrow icon. The purple back arrow icon in the top-left corner of the header is visually inconsistent with the other functional icons, which are black or gray. Change the back arrow icon's color from purple to the same dark gray used for the cart, user, and menu icons.

For clearer and more consistent communication of UI states, revise the tab styling. The active "Description" tab is styled with an underline, while the inactive "Curriculum" tab is styled with a light gray background, which is an inconsistent pattern. Remove the light gray background from the inactive "Curriculum" tab so that both tabs share a white background, and rely solely on the text color and underline to differentiate between active and inactive states.

To unify the visual language of the header, align the style of the primary navigation icons with the social media icons. Currently, the Facebook and Instagram icons in the yellow bar are a solid-fill style, while the shopping cart, user, and menu icons below them are an outline style. Change the shopping cart, user, and menu icons to a solid-fill style to match the social media icons, while keeping their existing dark gray color.
Improve Readability & Accessibility: The light gray text for the "Teacher" and "Category" labels provides insufficient contrast against the white background, making it difficult to read for users with visual impairments. Identify the "Teacher" label above the name "Sebastian Orth" and the "Category" label next to the thumbnail image. Change the text color for both of these labels from the current light gray to a darker, more accessible gray, such as #5A6064.

The inactive "Curriculum" tab has very low contrast, with light gray text on a light gray background, making it difficult to read and failing accessibility standards. Locate the tabbed navigation element below the course category information. Change the text color of the inactive "Curriculum" tab to a darker gray, such as #757575, to ensure it has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background.

The navigation icons in the header are a medium gray color that lacks strong visual prominence and may be difficult for users with low vision to see clearly. Locate the shopping cart, user profile, and hamburger menu icons in the top right of the screen, to the left of the social media icons. Change the color of all three icons to a darker, high-contrast shade, like #333333, to improve their visibility.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: Combine the "PRIME MEMBERS | Free shipping" banner with the promotional carousel to create a single, more cohesive Prime-focused content block. The current separation of these two elements weakens the overall Prime value proposition by splitting related information. Remove the dark blue banner containing the text "PRIME MEMBERS | Free shipping" and add the text "Free shipping for Prime members" directly below the "Discounts just for Prime Members" text inside the large blue carousel, using a white, regular-weight font that is 2pt smaller than the text above it.

Elevate the "Daily Deals" section above the Prime-exclusive promotional carousel to better reflect its importance as the site's core offering. The current hierarchy prioritizes a member-specific promotion over the primary content that appeals to all users, potentially hindering engagement for non-Prime visitors. Move the entire "Daily Deals" section, including its green heading, subtitle, and product listings, to be directly below the "PRIME MEMBERS | Free shipping" banner, placing it above the large blue carousel.

Reorganize the "Daily Deals" heading and its subtitle to create a stronger, more unified information hierarchy for the section. The current layout treats the subtitle "Our deepest discounts, one day only" as secondary information, despite its importance in conveying urgency. Combine these two text elements into a single visual component by placing the subtitle to the right of the heading on the same line. The heading "Daily Deals" should remain left-aligned, while the subtitle "Our deepest discounts, one day only." should be right-aligned, with its font size increased by 2pt to match the heading's visual weight.
Refine Layout & Spacing: To improve the visual separation between major page sections, add vertical spacing above the "Daily Deals" heading. The green "Daily Deals" heading and its gray subheading are currently flush against the blue "Prime Members" banner above them, which makes the layout feel cramped. Introduce a 24px top margin to the "Daily Deals" heading to create a distinct and balanced gap between the two sections.

The prices displayed under the "Daily Deals" product images are not vertically aligned, creating a visually jarring and unprofessional appearance in the product grid. To fix this, adjust the layout of the two price components ("$³⁹99 - $¹²³99" and "$¹⁵00") so that their text baselines are aligned on the same horizontal axis. This will create a more stable and organized row.

The text content within the blue "Discounts just for Prime Members" hero banner is not vertically centered, resulting in unbalanced negative space. To improve the banner's composition, vertically center the text block containing "PRIME Exclusive Store" and "Discounts just for Prime Members" within its blue container. This will ensure there is equal spacing between the top of the text block and the top edge of the banner, and the bottom of the text block and the carousel dots below.
Clarify Function & State: The pagination dots for the main content carousel do not clearly indicate the currently active slide, as the color difference between the active and inactive states is too subtle. To clarify the carousel's state, modify the pagination indicator located at the bottom of the blue "Prime Members" banner. Change the active indicator from a slightly darker blue circle to a solid blue, pill-shaped element that is twice the width of the inactive grey circular dots.

The two product listings under the "Daily Deals" heading lack visual cues to indicate that they are tappable, making their function as interactive elements ambiguous. To clarify their interactive potential, apply a style change to these product cards. Add a light grey 1-pixel solid border (#CCCCCC) and a subtle box-shadow (0px 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.1)) to each card to visually separate them from the background and suggest they are interactive components.

The text block within the blue "Prime Members" carousel is a tappable call-to-action, but its function is not immediately obvious because it lacks any standard interactive indicators. To clarify that this area is a link, add a white right-facing chevron icon (>) to the immediate right of the "Discounts just for Prime Members" text, signaling to the user that tapping this area will navigate them to a new page.
Add or Surface Functionality: To improve product discovery, add a search bar to the header, as users currently cannot search for items from the home screen. In the header section at the top of the screen, between the "woot!" logo and the shopping cart icon, insert a full-width, pill-shaped search field. The search field should have a white background, a light gray 1px border, a magnifying glass icon aligned to the left, and placeholder text that reads "Search products on Woot!".

To allow users to easily save deals for later consideration, add a "favorite" button to product listings. On each product card in the "Daily Deals" section (e.g., the sunglasses and clothing items), place a small heart outline icon in the top-right corner, overlaid on the product image. Tapping the icon should add the item to a user's favorites list and change the icon's appearance from an outline to a solid red heart.

To enable users to quickly find relevant offers, add category filters to the main deals section. In the space directly below the "Our deepest discounts, one day only" subheading and above the product listings, introduce a horizontally scrolling row of category filter chips. These chips should be labeled with popular categories like "Electronics," "Home & Kitchen," and "Fashion," have a light gray background with black text, and the selected chip should have a green background with white text.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: The "an amazon company" tagline in the header is redundant given the prominent Amazon Prime branding elsewhere on the page. Removing it will simplify the header and reduce visual clutter, allowing the Woot brand to stand out more clearly. The specific component to change is the gray text element that reads "an amazon company" positioned directly below the green "woot!" logo in the top-left of the screen. This text element should be removed entirely.

The subheading "Our deepest discounts, one day only." under the "Daily Deals" title is largely superfluous, as the main heading already implies time-sensitive offers. Removing this non-essential marketing copy will reduce text density and cognitive load. The component to be changed is the gray subtitle text located directly below the green "Daily Deals" heading. This subtitle should be removed, so the section consists of only the "Daily Deals" heading followed immediately by the product listings.

The current price format, which uses superscript for the cents value (e.g., "$15⁰⁰"), introduces unnecessary stylistic complexity and can be slightly less legible than a standard format. The price text for all items in the "Daily Deals" section, currently displaying prices like "$⁹⁹⁹⁹ – $¹²³⁹⁹" and "$¹⁵⁰⁰", should be modified. Update the price display style so that the cents are rendered at the same font size and on the same baseline as the dollar values, for example, changing "$15⁰⁰" to "$15.00".
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more cohesive color palette, the "Daily Deals" heading should use a brand color that's already established elsewhere on the screen. The current green color is not used in any other primary UI component, making it feel disconnected. Change the color of the "Daily Deals" heading text from green to the same dark blue-gray used for the background of the bottom navigation bar.

The price formatting in the "Daily Deals" section is inconsistent with standard, highly-legible e-commerce conventions. Using superscript for the cents can reduce readability, especially on smaller screens. Modify the price display for all items, such as "$¹⁵⁰⁰", so that the cents are rendered at the same font size and on the same baseline as the dollar amount (e.g., "$15.00").

To establish a more consistent typographic hierarchy, the text casing in the "PRIME MEMBERS" banner should match the title case used for other primary headings like "Daily Deals". Currently, the banner text is in all-caps, which clashes with the more common title case styling. Change the text in the dark blue-gray banner at the top of the screen from "PRIME MEMBERS | Free shipping" to "Prime Members | Free Shipping".
Improve Readability & Accessibility: To improve price legibility, the current format using superscript for the cents value is unconventional and can be difficult to read, especially for users with visual impairments. Change the price displays under the "Daily Deals" product images (e.g., "$15.00") so that the dollar sign, dollar value, and cents value are all vertically aligned on the same baseline and rendered at a consistent font size.

The light green text used for the "Daily Deals" heading has insufficient color contrast against the white background, which can make it difficult for users with low vision to perceive. Update the "Daily Deals" heading text by changing its color to a darker green, such as #3A773A, to ensure it meets the WCAG AA minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1.

Text set in all uppercase is less readable than mixed-case text because it eliminates the unique word shapes that aid in quick recognition. To improve scannability, modify the "PRIME MEMBERS | Free shipping" text in the dark blue banner below the header, changing it from all-caps to title case so it reads "Prime Members | Free Shipping".

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: To create a more logical flow, the call-to-action should follow the engaging visual content rather than precede it. Currently, the "Deals near you" banner is placed above the large image carousel, which disrupts the information hierarchy by presenting an action before its context. Move the light pink banner containing the text "Deals near you >" from its current position to the space immediately below the image carousel's pagination dots and above the "Try something new" section header.

The main heading "Time to crush it." is a generic slogan, while the actionable section "Try something new" is positioned further down the screen. To better guide the user toward discovery, the information hierarchy should prioritize actionable section titles over motivational phrases. Replace the text of the main "Time to crush it." heading with "Try something new," maintaining the same large, orange, centered style, and remove the "Try something new" heading and its orange underline from its original position below the carousel.

The "Deals near you" call-to-action is currently presented in a banner that is visually separate from the image it likely relates to, creating a disjointed information hierarchy. To create a more direct and compelling interactive element, the CTA should be merged with the primary visual. Remove the light pink banner labeled "Deals near you >" and instead overlay this text in a bold, white font onto the bottom-left corner of the image in the carousel, adding a subtle dark scrim behind the text to ensure legibility.
Refine Layout & Spacing: To improve visual balance and provide adequate breathing room, the main heading feels too close to the top of the screen. Add vertical spacing above the "Time to crush it." text heading at the top of the screen. The new top margin should be equal to the existing horizontal padding on the left side of the heading.

The proximity of the carousel pagination dots to the section title below them creates visual clutter and merges two distinct sections. To improve separation and scannability, increase the vertical space between these elements. Add 16px of top margin to the "Try something new" text heading, pushing it down from the four-dot carousel indicators directly above it.

The main content elements are not horizontally aligned, creating a visually inconsistent and untidy vertical axis. To establish a stronger visual structure, all content should share the same horizontal padding. Adjust the left padding for the "Time to crush it." heading and the "Deals near you" card so they align vertically with the "Try something new" heading below the image.
Clarify Function & State: The "Deals near you" banner's interactivity is ambiguous because both the text "Deals near you >" and the large image below it appear to be separate interactive elements. To clarify that the entire component is a single call-to-action, modify the banner located below the "Time to crush it" heading. Remove the "Deals near you >" text link from the top of the banner and instead, overlay that same text in a larger, white, bold font on top of the bottom-left corner of the image, perhaps with a subtle dark gradient behind the text for readability, making it clear the whole card is one tappable element.

The carousel state indicator does not clearly communicate the total number of items or the user's progress. To better clarify the user's position within the "Deals near you" carousel, replace the row of four circular dots located beneath the main image. In their place, implement a single, elongated pill-shaped progress bar that is 4px tall and spans 30% of the screen width, using a light grey (#E0E0E0) for the track and the current brand orange (#F05E35) for the progress fill, which should represent the current slide's position (e.g., 25% filled for slide 1 of 4).

The active state in the bottom navigation bar lacks sufficient visual prominence, making it less immediate for the user to identify which tab they are currently on. To clarify the user's location within the app, enhance the active state for the "HOME" item in the bottom navigation bar. In addition to its current orange color, place a solid, pill-shaped background fill, using the same brand orange color (#F05E35), behind the house icon only, ensuring the background has 8px of horizontal padding and 4px of vertical padding around the icon.
Add or Surface Functionality: To improve user control over location-based recommendations, the current location should be made visible and editable. In the light pink banner element under the "Time to crush it." heading, add a location pin icon and the user's detected city to the left of the "Deals near you" text. The updated text should read, for example, "[icon] Deals near Brooklyn >", making the location explicit and providing an entry point for changing it.

To allow users to save compelling offers for later, a favorite button should be added to the main content carousel. On the large image of the people working out, superimpose a white heart icon button in the top-right corner. The icon should have a semi-transparent dark circular background to ensure it is visible against various images.

To improve discoverability of content, an explicit navigation link should be provided for the "Try something new" section. To the right of the "Try something new" heading, add a new text link that reads "See all" in the app's primary orange accent color. This will create a clear call-to-action for users to explore all items in this category.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: To reduce visual clutter below the main image card, remove the carousel indicators. The component to be changed is the row of four circular dots located between the image of the people working out and the "Try something new" heading. The change is to completely remove this row of dots, which will result in the "Try something new" section shifting up slightly.

To simplify the main card element and reduce redundant information, remove the text banner overlaid on the image. The component to be removed is the light pink, rounded rectangle at the top of the image container that contains the text "Deals near you" and a right-facing arrow. After removing this banner, the image should extend to fill the full height of its container, up to the top rounded corners of the card.

To create a cleaner and less busy heading style, remove the decorative underline. The component to be changed is the short, thick orange line directly beneath the "Try something new" text. The heading text itself should remain in the same position, but the underline element should be completely removed.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more unified visual hierarchy for headings, the styling of the "Try something new" heading should be aligned with the primary heading. The heading element below the image carousel, which currently displays "Try something new" in black text with a red-orange underline, should be modified. Change its text color from black to the same red-orange used in the "Time to crush it." heading and remove the underline.

The light pink background on the "Deals near you" button is stylistically inconsistent with the otherwise clean, white-background design of the screen. To create a more cohesive look, the rectangular container with rounded corners located below the "Time to crush it." heading should be updated. Remove the light pink background fill, making the container's background transparent to match the main page background.

The color used for inactive states is inconsistent between the carousel pagination and the bottom navigation, which can weaken the app's visual language. The three inactive pagination dots, currently light grey and located below the image carousel, should be updated for consistency. Change their fill color from light grey to the same medium grey used for the inactive icons and text in the bottom navigation bar (e.g., "SEARCH," "FAVORITES").
Improve Readability & Accessibility: To improve accessibility, increase the contrast of the inactive navigation items. In the bottom navigation bar, the "SEARCH", "FAVORITES", "DEALS", and "PROFILE" icons and labels are currently a light gray. Change the color of these four inactive icons and their corresponding text labels to a darker gray, such as #757575, to ensure they meet WCAG AA contrast ratio standards against the white background.

To enhance readability for all users, the text color of the "Deals near you" link needs to be darkened. This link, located in the light pink container above the main image, currently uses an orange text color that has insufficient contrast with its background. Update the text color to a darker shade of orange, like #D9502D, to achieve a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1.

To make carousel navigation more accessible, the visibility of the inactive indicator dots must be improved. The three light gray inactive dots, located below the main image and to the right of the active orange dot, have very low contrast against the white background. Change the color of these inactive dots from light gray to a darker gray, like #BDBDBD, to make them more perceptible.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: The "No speed camera in front of you" banner currently commands the most attention due to its size and high placement, which misrepresents its importance as a non-critical status update. To improve the information hierarchy, this banner should be de-emphasized so the user can focus on the primary map view. Identify the large, light-blue banner at the top of the screen and reduce its height by 40%. Relocate it to the bottom of the screen, positioned directly above the advertisement, and change its background to a semi-transparent dark gray (#2F2F2F with 80% opacity).

The user's current speed is a primary piece of data in this application, but its location in the bottom-left corner places it in a secondary position within the visual hierarchy. To better reflect its importance, the speedometer should be moved to a more prominent, easily scannable location. Move the large, blue circular speedometer element from the bottom-left corner to the top of the screen, centered horizontally, and positioned directly below the "Live Radar" header. This elevates the most critical real-time information to a primary focal point.

The speedometer and the re-center map button are the main information display and user control, respectively, but their scattered placement creates a disjointed layout. To create a more logical information structure, these related components should be grouped into a single control cluster. Identify the circular speedometer in the bottom-left and the square re-center button in the bottom-right, and move the re-center button to be positioned directly above the speedometer in the bottom-left corner, with a 12-pixel vertical margin between them.
Refine Layout & Spacing: The primary notification banner at the top of the screen is positioned too close to the header bar, and its internal padding is insufficient, making the UI feel cramped. To improve visual separation and readability, increase the top margin of the blue "No speed camera in front of you" banner to 16dp and increase its internal vertical padding (both top and bottom) by 6dp.

The speedometer in the bottom-left corner is positioned too close to the edges of the screen, creating a cluttered and unbalanced layout. To give the element more breathing room and improve the overall composition, increase the margins for the circular blue speedometer element to 24dp from both the left and bottom screen edges.

The icons stacked vertically on the right side of the map lack consistent alignment and spacing, making the layout appear disorganized. To create a more structured and visually appealing arrangement, right-align the orange restaurant icon, the teal home icon, and the white square "re-center" button. Additionally, standardize the vertical space between each of these three elements to 20dp.
Clarify Function & State: To clarify the user's current state beyond just location, the user icon on the map should also indicate their direction of travel. The current user location marker, a simple blue circle with a white outline on the highway, should be updated. Replace the circle with a blue, filled-in, chevron-style arrow icon that points in the direction the user is currently moving, making it immediately clear which way is "in front of you."

The top banner's function as a status indicator is unclear because its blue color makes it look like a header or a button. The large, blue, rounded rectangular banner at the top of the screen containing the text "No speed camera in front of you" needs to be restyled to better communicate a safe, "all-clear" status. Change the background color of this banner from blue to a standard success/safe green (e.g., #28A745) to make its meaning as a positive status update instantly recognizable.

The function of the floating icons on the map is ambiguous, as it is unclear whether they are tappable points of interest or just static map decorations. To clarify that these icons are interactive, add a subtle drop shadow to the orange restaurant icon, the teal home icon, and the green running person icon. This will give the icons a sense of depth and affordance, visually suggesting they can be tapped to reveal more information.
Add or Surface Functionality: To allow users to contribute real-time road conditions and improve the utility of the map for all drivers, add a reporting feature. In the bottom-right corner of the screen, add a new circular, orange floating action button with a white plus icon. This new button should be positioned directly above the existing white "re-center" button.

To provide users with more control over the map's appearance and readability, surface a control for changing the map type. Add a new square, white icon button, styled identically to the "re-center" button in the bottom right. This new button should be placed directly above the re-center button and should contain a map layers icon (a stack of three squares), which, when tapped, would allow the user to toggle between satellite, terrain, and standard map views.

To provide users with essential at-a-glance information about their current route, add a persistent trip details display. In the top-right corner of the screen, below the header bar, add a semi-transparent dark gray box with white text. This box should display three key pieces of information vertically: remaining distance to the destination, estimated time of arrival (ETA), and remaining travel time.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: To reduce visual obstruction of the map, the large notification banner at the top of the screen should only be displayed when an alert is active. The component to change is the large, opaque blue banner labeled "No speed camera in front of you." This banner should be removed entirely from its default state, allowing users an unobstructed view of the map when no cameras are detected nearby.

The map is cluttered with multiple points of interest that may distract from the app's primary function of radar detection. To simplify the interface, remove the non-essential, colorful icons by default. The specific components to remove are the orange fork-and-knife icon, the teal home icon, and the green walking person icon, freeing up map space and reducing cognitive load.

The speedometer in the bottom-left corner is overly large and visually dominant, creating unnecessary clutter. To make it less intrusive, modify the large blue circle element that currently displays "0 km/h." Reduce its overall diameter by 40% and change its solid blue background to a semi-transparent blue (70% opacity) to allow the map to be seen through it.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more unified visual language for informational components, the corner radius of the main status banner should be made consistent with other key elements. The large, light blue banner at the top of the screen that reads "No speed camera in front of you" currently has a fully rounded, pill-shaped appearance. Update this banner by reducing its corner radius to match the more subtle rounding of the "Download" button in the advertisement at the bottom of the screen.

The styling for tappable icons is inconsistent, creating a disjointed user experience. The search icon in the top-right corner is a simple outline, whereas the re-center map button in the bottom-right corner is a filled icon inside a distinct white container. To create a consistent visual style for primary action icons, modify the search icon by placing it inside a white, square container with rounded corners, identical to the container used for the re-center map button.

The application's color palette lacks cohesion due to the use of multiple, slightly different shades of blue for prominent UI elements. The large speedometer circle in the bottom-left corner uses a blue gradient, while the informational banner at the top ("No speed camera in front of you") uses a solid, lighter blue. Change the background of the top information banner from the solid light blue to the same blue gradient used for the speedometer, creating a more consistent and intentional color scheme.
Improve Readability & Accessibility: To improve the legibility of points of interest on the map, the text label for the "Computer History Museum" needs better contrast against the complex satellite imagery. Locate the small, grey text label "Computer History Museum" in the center-right of the map view. Change the text color to white and apply a subtle, dark text-shadow (e.g., 1px 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.7)) to lift it from the background, ensuring it is readable regardless of the map details behind it.

The descriptive text in the ad at the bottom of the screen has insufficient color contrast, making it difficult for users with visual impairments to read. The light grey text "A radar detector for police detector GPS speedometer." on a white background in the ad banner at the bottom fails accessibility guidelines. Change the color of this text to a darker grey, specifically #4F4F4F, to meet a minimum AA contrast ratio of 4.5:1.

The primary status message at the top of the screen can be difficult to read because the white text lacks separation from the varied colors of the map below it. The text "No speed camera in front of you" within the large blue banner should be made more distinct. Add a subtle, dark drop shadow to this text (e.g., 1px vertical offset, 2px blur, color rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5)) to enhance its readability against the busy map background.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: The notification permission request immediately interrupts the user's primary task, creating a disjointed initial experience by prioritizing a secondary action over core app engagement. To improve the user flow, the modal titled "Don't miss out on the latest deals & order status updates" which overlays the main screen should be removed from the initial app launch. Instead, this permission request should be triggered after the user completes a high-value action for the first time, such as placing an order or clipping a coupon, to present the request in a more logical and contextually relevant moment.

The four primary action icons are currently positioned below the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner, diminishing their hierarchical importance and suggesting they are secondary to selecting a shopping mode. To establish these as global, top-level navigation elements, the row of four light gray circular icon buttons (location pin, grid with plus, credit card, barcode) should be relocated into the main header. They should be positioned to the left of the existing list and shopping cart icons, making them persistent and more prominent.

The "Choose Pickup or Delivery" call-to-action is a critical first step, but it is visually disconnected from the search bar, which is the primary tool for acting on that choice. To create a more logical information hierarchy that guides the user from mode selection to action, the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner, located below the header, should be moved up and integrated directly beneath the "Search Products..." bar. This change visually groups the shopping context with the primary tool for shopping, reinforcing a clear "select mode, then search" user flow.
Refine Layout & Spacing: Refine the layout of the notification permission modal by adjusting the button container's padding for better visual balance. The two action buttons, "NOT NOW" and "SOUNDS GREAT," are currently positioned flush with the bottom and side edges of the modal, making them feel cramped. Add 16dp of padding to the left, right, and bottom of the container that holds these two buttons, creating a clear visual margin between the buttons and the modal's border.

Improve the vertical spacing within the notification permission modal to create a more organized and readable layout. The spacing between the title ("Don't miss out..."), the body text ("Allow notifications..."), and the button container is currently tight and uneven. Increase the vertical margin between the bottom of the body text and the top of the button container to 24dp to give the content more room to breathe.

Adjust the spacing of the four circular icon buttons to improve their visual grouping and reduce excessive negative space. The four icons (location pin, grid, credit card, barcode) located beneath the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner are spread too far apart, making them feel disconnected. Reduce the horizontal space between each icon so that the entire group is more compact, occupying approximately 70% of the screen width and centered horizontally.
Clarify Function & State: The function of the four circular icons below the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner is ambiguous without labels. Add a short, descriptive text label directly below each of the four icons (location pin, grid with plus, credit card, barcode). The labels should clearly state the function of each button, such as "Stores", "Departments", "Wallet", and "Scan", using a legible font color that contrasts with the background.

It is unclear which of the category filter buttons (e.g., "Summer Celebration," "Back to School") is currently active, as they all share the same visual style. To clarify the current state, change the background color of the selected filter button to the primary Meijer blue (#004E8A) and its text to white. This will visually distinguish the active filter from the inactive gray ones, providing immediate feedback to the user about their selection.

In the "Don't miss out..." notification pop-up, the primary action ("SOUNDS GREAT") and the secondary action ("NOT NOW") have identical visual weight, which fails to guide the user. To clarify the intended primary action, convert the "SOUNDS GREAT" text link into a solid, filled button using the app's primary blue background color and white text. Leave the "NOT NOW" option as a simple text link to create a clear visual hierarchy.
Add or Surface Functionality: To improve the discoverability of the in-app scanner, add a clarifying text label to its icon. The barcode icon, which is the rightmost of the four circular icons under the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner, is ambiguous. Add the text label "Scan" directly underneath this icon, centered horizontally, using the same font style and color as other minor text in the app.

To help users plan their shopping trips more efficiently, surface the selected store's hours directly on the home screen. The "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner below the search bar currently only displays that text. Add a new line of smaller text beneath "Choose Pickup or Delivery" that reads "Open today until [closing time]" or "Closed," dynamically pulling the hours for the user's currently selected store.

To provide returning users with faster access to frequently purchased products, add a "Favorites" button to the main header. The top-right of the header currently contains a view toggle, a list icon, and a cart icon. Insert a new heart-shaped "Favorites" icon to the left of the shopping cart icon, matching the style and size of the other icons in the row.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: To reduce visual clutter and simplify the primary navigation area, the row of four secondary action icons should be removed. This row, located directly below the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" bar, contains four circular, light gray icons: a location pin, a grid with a plus symbol, a credit card, and a barcode. Remove this entire row of icons, and shift the category filter buttons ("Summer Celebration", "Back to School", etc.) up to fill the resulting space.

The body text in the notification permission modal is too long, increasing cognitive load by listing too many specific benefits. The paragraph of text in the white modal popup, which currently reads "Allow notifications & stay up-to-date on order status, mPerks coupons, rewards progress and more," should be shortened to simplify the message. Change this text to read: "Allow notifications to get order updates and exclusive deals."

The search bar in the top navigation header is cluttered with an unnecessary icon that creates confusion. The 2x2 grid icon, currently located inside the far right of the "Search Products..." input field, should be removed entirely. After this change, the search input field should only contain the magnifying glass icon on the left and the placeholder text.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To improve typographical consistency across interactive elements, the text casing of the modal buttons should match the casing used in the filter chips. The two buttons at the bottom of the "Don't miss out..." modal, labeled "NOT NOW" and "SOUNDS GREAT," currently use all-caps text. Update these button labels to use title case, so they read "Not Now" and "Sounds Great."

The row of four circular icon buttons below the "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner lacks visual consistency by mixing solid and outline icon styles. The location pin and barcode icons are solid-filled, while the grid and credit card icons are outlines. To unify their appearance, replace the solid-fill location pin and barcode icons with new outline-style versions that match the line weight and style of the adjacent grid and credit card icons.

The "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner feels disconnected from the rest of the interface due to its sharp corners, which contrast with the rounded corners used on the search bar, filter chips, and modal. This inconsistency disrupts the visual harmony of the screen's container elements. To fix this, apply a corner radius to the light grey "Choose Pickup or Delivery" banner that is consistent with the corner radius of the search bar above it.
Improve Readability & Accessibility: Increase the color contrast of the search bar's placeholder text to improve legibility for users with visual impairments. The placeholder text "Search Products..." in the search bar at the top of the screen currently has insufficient contrast against its blue background. Change the color of this placeholder text from its current light gray to white (#FFFFFF) to meet WCAG accessibility standards.

Enhance the visibility of the secondary action button in the permissions modal to better define its tappable area. The "NOT NOW" button within the "Don't miss out..." modal currently has a thin, light-colored border that is difficult to perceive. Increase the button's border width to 2px and change its color to the same dark blue used for the button's text (#004C97).

Improve the readability of the body text within the notification permission modal by increasing its color contrast. The paragraph of text beginning "Allow notifications..." in the center of the modal uses a medium gray color that does not meet the WCAG AAA standard for contrast. Change the text color from its current gray (approximately #5A5A5A) to a darker gray, such as #444444, to ensure a contrast ratio of at least 7:1 against the white background.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: To better distinguish the primary action from the secondary recovery option, the "Trouble Logging in?" link should be de-emphasized. Currently, the row containing the question mark icon and "Trouble Logging in? Tap Here" text has a high visual weight comparable to the input fields. This row should be replaced with a simple, centered text link styled in the same font as the field labels, reading "Trouble Logging in?", positioned directly below the "LOG IN" button.

To focus the user on the primary task of logging in, the notice about interest-based advertising, which is tertiary information, should be moved out of the main content flow. The row containing the 'i' icon and the text "Tap here to access device settings for opting-out of interest-based advertising and services" should be relocated to the very bottom of the screen. The icon should be removed and the text font size reduced to establish this as a footer element, separate from the core login functionality.

To create a clearer hierarchy that prioritizes the login form itself, the redundant "Log In" header should be removed, as the context is already clear from the form's content. The entire top navigation bar containing the back arrow and the "Log In" text should be removed. All other content on the screen should be shifted up to occupy the newly available space, making the "Email, phone, or username" field the topmost element.
Refine Layout & Spacing: To improve the visual hierarchy and rhythm of the form, the spacing between the input fields and the primary action button feels too tight. Increase the top margin of the light green "LOG IN" button by approximately 50% to create more deliberate separation from the "Password" input field directly above it.

The vertical alignment of the icons and input fields is imbalanced, making the layout feel unpolished. Realign the person and lock icons, currently centered with the input lines. Instead, vertically align the top of each icon with the baseline of its corresponding text label ("Email, phone, or username" and "Password").

The primary form elements lack sufficient horizontal padding, making the layout feel crowded against the screen edges. Apply a consistent horizontal padding to the "Email, phone, or username" field, the "Password" field, and the "LOG IN" button. The new padding should align the start and end of these elements with the start and end of the "Trouble Logging in? Tap Here" text block below them.
Clarify Function & State: The "LOG IN" button appears active regardless of whether the input fields are filled, which fails to clearly communicate when the login action is possible. To clarify its state, the large, light green "LOG IN" button below the password field should be disabled by default. In its disabled state, change the button's background color to a desaturated light gray (e.g., #E0E0E0) and the text color to a lighter gray (e.g., #BDBDBD). The button should only switch to its current active green appearance and become tappable once both the username and password fields contain input.

The password field lacks a way for users to view their input, making it difficult to check for typos and increasing the potential for login errors. To clarify the input state, add an icon button inside the password field, aligned to the right. This icon should toggle the visibility of the typed password; use an "eye" icon to represent the "show password" state and a "slashed eye" icon for the "hide password" state, updating the icon each time the user taps it.

The "Trouble Logging in? Tap Here" link uses a generic and redundant call to action, which can be confusing. To clarify its function, revise this link, located below the "LOG IN" button. Remove the "Tap Here" text and its underline, and instead make the entire phrase "Trouble Logging in?" the tappable element, styling it with the same underline and font weight as the original "Tap Here" text.
Add or Surface Functionality: To improve usability and reduce login errors, add a password visibility toggle. In the "Password" input field, which is located below the "Email, phone, or username" field, add an eye icon button aligned to the right side. Tapping this icon should toggle the password's visibility between masked characters and plain text, with the icon itself switching between an open eye and a slashed eye to indicate the current state.

To provide a more direct path for new user registration, a sign-up link should be added to this screen. In the space below the green "LOG IN" button and above the "Trouble Logging in?" section, insert a new line of text that reads "Don't have an account? **Sign Up**". The "Sign Up" portion should be styled as a hyperlink that navigates the user to the account creation flow.

To make account recovery more specific and efficient, the generic help link should be replaced with targeted options. Replace the "Trouble Logging in? Tap Here" text and question mark icon with two distinct, horizontally-aligned text links: "Forgot Username?" and "Forgot Password?". These links should be positioned directly below the "LOG IN" button, providing clear, discoverable paths for common login issues.
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: To reduce textual clutter, revise the "Trouble Logging in?" link by removing the explicit call to action. The component in question is the link below the "LOG IN" button, currently reading "Trouble Logging in? Tap Here". Change this text to simply "Trouble Logging in?" and make this entire new phrase the tappable element, which will perform the same action as the original link.

To create a cleaner and more focused input area, remove the redundant icons from the login fields. The components to be changed are the green person icon to the left of the "Email, phone, or username" field and the gray lock icon to the left of the "Password" field. Remove both icons and align the text labels ("Email, phone, or username" and "Password") to the left edge where the icons used to be.

To simplify the screen and remove information not directly relevant to the primary task of logging in, remove the section about interest-based advertising. This component is the text block below the "Trouble Logging in?" link that reads "Tap here to access device settings for opting-out of interest-based advertising and services," along with its accompanying circular info icon. Remove this entire element, including both the icon and the text.
Strengthen Visual Consistency: To create a more unified visual language, the icons on the screen should be stylistically consistent. The user and password icons are solid glyphs, while the "Trouble Logging in?" and device settings icons are outlined and enclosed in circles. Replace all four icons (person, lock, question mark, info) with a single, consistent set of outlined icons with a uniform stroke weight and no enclosing circles.

The capitalization of interactive text elements is inconsistent, which can make the UI feel disjointed. The main "LOG IN" button in the center of the screen uses all-caps, whereas the "Tap Here" link below it uses title case. Change the button text from "LOG IN" to "Log In" to match the capitalization style of the link and the page title for better consistency.

The color used for the primary action button is inconsistent with the accent color used elsewhere for active elements. The "LOG IN" button has a muted, light green background, while the active input field state is indicated with a more saturated, darker green. Change the background color of the "LOG IN" button to match the darker green of the active "Email, phone, or username" label and change the button's text color to white to ensure readability.
Improve Readability & Accessibility: The green "Email, phone, or username" label has insufficient color contrast against the light background, making it difficult for users with visual impairments to read. Change the color of the "Email, phone, or username" text label above the first input field from the current green to a dark gray (#4A4A4A) to meet WCAG AA contrast standards.

The light gray "Password" placeholder text has very poor contrast against the white input field background, rendering it nearly illegible for many users. To improve readability and meet accessibility guidelines, change the color of the "Password" placeholder text in the second input field from its current light gray to a medium gray, #757575.

The white "LOG IN" text on the light green button does not provide enough contrast, which can make the primary call to action difficult for users with low vision to read. Increase the background color saturation of the "LOG IN" button to a darker green, such as #6BAA62, to ensure the white text achieves a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1.

Reorganize Information Hierarchy: To clarify the primary call to action and reduce visual clutter at the bottom of the screen, the two separate informational bars should be consolidated. Identify the dark grey bar containing the text "Touch on map for new location" and the blue bar beneath it reading "More Just4Fun for you." Remove the dark grey bar entirely and change the text within the bottom blue bar to "Touch on map for new location," centralizing this key instruction and removing the secondary, distracting message.

The primary action "play" button currently floats over the map, competing with map content and the bottom UI elements for attention. To create a clearer hierarchy between map content and application controls, this action should be moved to the main control bar. Remove the large pink circular floating action button with the play icon from the bottom-right corner of the screen. Add a new play icon button to the right of the "Search for location..." text in the top navigation bar, creating a single, dedicated area for all primary UI controls.

To better guide the user toward the most common starting action, the search functionality needs to be more prominent in the information hierarchy of the top bar. Currently, the hamburger menu icon in the top-left corner competes with the adjacent search bar. Remove the hamburger menu icon and expand the "Search for location..." bar to occupy the full width of the header, making search the primary, unambiguous function of this top-level component.
Refine Layout & Spacing: To improve usability and visual balance in the top navigation bar, adjust the spacing of its contents. The hamburger menu icon, located in the top-left corner, and the search icon, located to the left of the search field, are currently too close to the screen edge and the text, respectively. Add 16dp of padding to the left of the hamburger icon and increase the space between the search icon and the "Search for location..." placeholder text to 12dp.

The text in the bottom instructional banner is not vertically centered, creating a slightly unbalanced appearance. To fix this, vertically center the text "Touch on map for new location." within its dark grey container banner, which is located just above the purple "More Just4Fun for you" button. This will ensure consistent alignment and a more polished look.

The text labels on the map, such as "Shoreline Amphitheatre" and "Computer History Museum," are positioned too close to their corresponding map markers, which creates visual clutter. To improve readability, increase the vertical spacing between all map labels and their associated icon markers by 8dp. This added buffer will help distinguish the text from the icons.
Clarify Function & State: The function of the various solid-colored teardrop pins (grey, green, teal) is unclear, as they lack any iconography to suggest what type of location they represent. To clarify their purpose, add a distinct, universally recognizable white icon to the center of each of these pins. For instance, a star icon could represent saved places, a building icon for landmarks, and a tree icon for parks, making the map easier to interpret at a glance.

The purpose of the large pink button in the bottom-right corner is ambiguous because its play icon could be interpreted as starting a video tour or initiating turn-by-turn navigation. Change the white play icon inside this circular pink button to a standard directions icon, such as a right-turning arrow or a stylized car. This will clearly communicate that the button's function is to start navigation to the selected location.

While the red pin indicates a specific location, its state as the active selection is not explicitly confirmed with any corresponding information. Add a small information card or banner anchored to the bottom of the screen, just above the "Touch on map..." bar, that displays the name or address of the location currently marked by the red pin. This will clarify the pin's state as the selected destination and provide immediate context.
Add or Surface Functionality: To allow users to easily orient themselves, add a "re-center on my location" button, a standard feature currently missing from the map interface. This new circular floating action button should be placed directly above the existing pink "play" button in the bottom right corner. The button should have a white background and contain a blue "crosshairs" or "target" icon, and tapping it should animate the map to the user's current GPS location.

To make selecting a point on the map more informative, replace the content of the bottom banner when a location is selected with the red pin. The current banner, which reads "Touch on map for new location," provides no context about the selected point. Instead, this banner should display the name and/or address of the pinned location, along with primary action buttons like "Directions" and "Save."

Add a map layer switcher to allow users to toggle between different map views, as the current satellite-only view may not be suitable for all use cases. Introduce a new floating icon button in the top-right corner of the map, below the search bar. This button should feature a "layers" icon (stacked squares) and, when tapped, should present a menu with options to select map types such as "Default," "Satellite," and "Terrain."
Simplify & Reduce Clutter: To reduce distraction from the primary map view, remove the promotional banner at the bottom of the screen. The component to be changed is the solid blue banner at the very bottom of the UI containing the text "More Just4Fun for you". This entire banner should be removed, leaving only the dark gray instructional banner above it.

The map is cluttered with an excessive number of generic location pins and labels, which makes it visually noisy and difficult to parse. To simplify the view, remove all non-essential point-of-interest markers from the map. This includes the two green pins, one teal pin, and four gray pins, as well as their associated text labels (e.g., "Shoreline Amphitheatre", "Computer History Museum"), leaving only the primary red pin and functional icons like the parking and restaurant symbols.
